attrs.alt: Mob Programming Session — roles for agent feature work

content.text: What Mob Programming Adds to GenAI Work

Mob programming — where the entire team works together on one task at one computer — is an extreme form of collaborative development. For standard software, the typical justification is rapid knowledge transfer and higher defect detection. For agentic AI features, mob programming offers a third benefit: collective evaluation of non-deterministic behaviour.

When a model's response is displayed on the shared screen, every member of the mob sees it simultaneously and can comment on whether it matches the expected behaviour. This parallel observation catches subtle quality issues that a single developer reviewing their own output would miss: an appropriate response that has the wrong tone, a refusal that is technically correct but unnecessarily hostile, or a context-retention failure visible only when a mob member remembers the earlier turn.

GenAI mob sessions also serve as an accelerated onboarding mechanism. Because all code and prompt changes happen in front of the entire team, every team member understands the architecture, the prompt's design decisions, and the safety constraints from the start. There is no knowledge silo to create.
Standard Mob Roles Adapted for Agent Work
Standard mob programming defines two roles — driver and navigator. For GenAI agent feature work, two additional specialist roles improve the session quality significantly:
Safety Observer:

tracks whether changes to the system prompt or the integration code could introduce new harm categories, weaken refusal conditions, or bypass the PII masking layer. The safety observer has authority to call a review pause at any time when they see a potential safety regression.
Eval Curator:

maintains the eval suite during the session. When a failure mode is discovered through manual testing, the eval curator writes the corresponding eval case immediately — before the team moves on. This ensures that discoveries made during the mob session are captured as permanent regression tests.
Cost Watcher:
tracks the token count per API call as the integration is built. Any change that materially increases the token count triggers a two-minute cost impact review with the full mob before proceeding.
Prompt Owner:

for sessions involving system prompt changes, one mob member tracks the approved prompt hash and flags any unapproved change before it is committed. In some teams this role is combined with the safety observer.
The 15-Minute Round Structure

Mob programming sessions for GenAI work use 15-minute rounds rather than the 25-minute rounds more common in standard mob sessions. The shorter round length is justified by the higher rate of context-switching: in GenAI work, each round may involve writing code, running evals, reviewing model output, and making a prompt change. Shorter rounds maintain energy and ensure that all team members rotate through the driver and navigator roles.
Start of each round:
the incoming driver and navigator align on the one goal for this round (not three goals — one). The mob members agree or suggest a change. This takes 60 seconds maximum.
Mid-round eval run:
if a change is made to the system prompt or integration code, the eval curator triggers an eval suite run immediately. The mob pauses to review the output rather than continuing to implement.
End of round:
the driver announces the current state: eval suite status (N passing, N failing), any prompt changes made, and the one thing the incoming driver should focus on. This handoff takes 90 seconds.
Role rotation:

all roles rotate at the end of each round, not just driver and navigator. The safety observer and eval curator roles rotate with the other positions so that each team member develops familiarity with all perspectives.
The Full Session Agenda
Use this agenda for a three-hour mob session building a GenAI agent feature from design to integrated code:

# mob-session-agenda-agent-feature.md
# 3-hour mob session for building a GenAI agent feature.
# Team: 4-6 engineers including at least one with prompt engineering experience
#       and one with safety review background.

## Pre-Session Setup (15 min before start)
1. Confirm approved system prompt hash is loaded on the main machine.
2. Confirm eval suite is passing on main branch (clean baseline).
3. Open three windows: editor, eval runner, API response viewer.
4. Assign initial roles:
   - Driver: [name]
   - Navigator: [name]
   - Safety observer: [name]
   - Eval curator: [name]
   - Cost watcher: [name]

## Hour 1: Core Integration (15-min rounds x 4)
Goal: agent correctly handles the primary use case end-to-end.

Round 1: Driver writes the basic API client integration.
  Navigator: "First, let's make sure we're calling the right endpoint and handling the auth."
  Eval curator: run the 3 baseline eval cases and verify they still pass.

Round 2: Driver integrates the system prompt.
  Safety observer: "The system prompt file we're using — is that the approved hash from the RACI?"
  Mob: verify hash before proceeding.

Round 3: Driver writes the primary user flow end-to-end.
  Cost watcher: count tokens in each test call. "We're at 850 tokens per call. Budget was 1200."
  Navigator: think ahead to error handling next round.

Round 4: Driver adds error handling (timeout, rate limit, empty response).
  Eval curator: write 3 eval cases for error conditions.
  ROTATE ALL ROLES.

## Hour 2: Edge Cases and Eval Expansion (15-min rounds x 4)
Goal: eval suite grows to cover all identified failure modes.

Round 5: Driver implements context retention across turns.
  Safety observer: "Are we at risk of context leaking between test sessions in the eval runner?"
  Eval curator: write multi-turn eval cases.

Round 6: Driver implements scope boundary enforcement (refusal conditions).
  Safety observer: "The refusal text we just wrote — does it match what's in the approved prompt spec?"
  Mob: review against PRD section 3.2.

Round 7: Driver implements PII masking.
  Safety observer: "Let's run a manual test where we input an email address and verify it doesn't appear in the API call."
  Cost watcher: PII masking layer adds 0 tokens (it's a pre-processing step — confirm).

Round 8: Driver implements HITL confirmation for high-stakes action.
  Eval curator: write 2 eval cases for HITL trigger conditions.
  ROTATE ALL ROLES.

## Hour 3: Integration Tests and Handoff (15-min rounds x 4)
Goal: complete eval suite passing, commit, handoff note written.

Round 9-10: Eval curator leads — write eval cases for every failure mode discovered in hours 1-2.
  Target: eval suite has at least 20 cases at end of this round.
  Driver implements fixes for any new cases that fail.

Round 11: Full eval suite run on the final state.
  Target: 100% passing. If not: diagnose and fix before round 12.
  Cost watcher: final token count per session. Update cost estimate in PRD if changed.

Round 12: Handoff
  Safety observer: confirm prompt hash and change log are up to date.
  Navigator: write commit message following convention.
  Driver: commit and push.
  Full mob: write handoff note for next session.

## Post-Session
  [ ] Eval suite passing on main branch
  [ ] Change log updated (if prompt changed)
  [ ] Cost estimate updated (if token count changed)
  [ ] Handoff note filed in project wiki
  [ ] Next mob session scheduled
Mob Retrospective for GenAI Sessions
Run a 15-minute retrospective at the end of every mob session. The GenAI-specific retrospective adds safety and eval questions to the standard format:

# mob-retrospective-template-genai.md
# Run this at the end of every mob session on agent feature work.
# 15 minutes max. Go around the group for each question.

## What we built this session
  Feature/story completed:
  Eval cases added: [N] (was [N] at start, [N] at end)
  System prompt changes: [N] (Minor: [N], Major: [N], Safety: [N])
  Token cost per session: [before] -> [after]

## What went well
  (Each person names one thing. No duplicates.)
  1.
  2.
  3.
  4.

## What we would do differently
  (Each person names one thing. Facilitator adds to backlog if actionable.)
  1.
  2.
  3.
  4.

## Safety-Specific Review
  Was there a moment when we almost committed an unapproved prompt change? YES / NO
  Was there a moment when the safety observer raised a concern that changed our approach? YES / NO
  Did we find any failure mode that is NOT covered by an eval case? YES / NO
    If YES: write the eval case right now before leaving.

## Carry-Forwards
  (Items to address at next mob session start)
  [ ] [item 1]
  [ ] [item 2]
Decision-Making in the Mob

The mob's power comes from collective intelligence, but collective decision-making without a protocol produces either paralysis (no one can agree) or false consensus (everyone agrees to avoid conflict). Establish a clear decision protocol before the session starts.
Prompt change protocol:
any proposed change to the system prompt must be stated aloud by the navigator, evaluated by the safety observer, and agreed by the mob before the driver types it. No silent prompt changes.
Eval case disputes:

if a mob member disputes whether a failing case represents a real failure mode, the eval curator has final authority. The disputed case is added to a 'review later' list and is not allowed to block the session.
Cost veto:
the cost watcher has authority to call a two-minute pause if a change increases estimated session cost by more than 10%. The pause is not a veto — it is a mandatory impact review before proceeding.
Safety veto:

the safety observer has authority to call a full stop if they identify a potential safety regression. This is not a discussion — it is a stop. The issue must be resolved before the mob resumes. This authority is only used for genuine safety concerns, not as a general veto.
When Mob Programming Works Best
Complex agent architecture decisions:

the design of the agent orchestration layer, the context management strategy, and the tool call framework are decisions that benefit from collective intelligence. A mob session for these decisions produces better architecture than any individual developer working alone.
Safety-critical code:
PII masking, session isolation, and HITL logic are too important to be written by one person without peer review. Writing them in a mob session provides immediate review with no lag.
Onboarding:
the first sprint of a new feature is the ideal time for a mob session. Every team member develops the context they need to work independently in subsequent sprints.
Eval suite expansion:
writing eval cases for a new feature's full failure mode catalogue is a mob-appropriate task because different team members will think of different failure modes.

Mob programming is not appropriate for all work. Routine integration work, documentation, and boilerplate code are better handled by individuals or pairs. Reserve mob sessions for the highest-value, highest-risk tasks where collective intelligence justifies the coordination cost.
Mob Sessions for Eval Suite Expansion

One of the highest-value mob session formats for GenAI work is the eval expansion session — a focused 90-minute session where the entire mob works together to write eval cases for every identified failure mode. This session format is particularly effective because different team members think of different failure modes, and the collective coverage is dramatically broader than any individual could achieve.

The eval expansion session has a specific structure: each round targets a failure mode category (scope, context, safety, error handling), and each mob member contributes cases from their perspective. The driver adds all cases to the eval suite in real time, and the suite is run at the end of each round. Any case that fails triggers immediate diagnosis and fix before the mob moves to the next category.

# mob-eval-expansion-session.md
# A 90-minute focused mob session for expanding the eval suite.
# Use this when the current eval suite covers fewer than 80% of identified failure modes.

## Pre-Session (10 min)
1. List all failure modes identified in the Safety Impact Assessment.
2. List all failure modes discovered in prototype testing.
3. List all failure modes discovered in previous mob sessions.
4. Tally: how many have an eval case? Which are missing?

## Round 1 — Scope and Refusal Eval Cases (20 min)
Each mob member writes 2 eval cases from this list:
  - Primary in-scope task: does the agent complete it in <= expected turns?
  - Edge of scope: does the agent handle a request that is 80% in-scope correctly?
  - Out-of-scope refusal: does the agent refuse with the approved phrasing?
  - Out-of-scope with redirect: does the refusal include a useful redirect?
  - Nested scope: an in-scope request embedded in an out-of-scope context?
Driver adds all cases to the eval suite. Run the suite. All new cases should pass.
Fix failures before moving to Round 2.

## Round 2 — Context and Memory Eval Cases (20 min)
Each mob member writes 2 eval cases from this list:
  - Name retention: agent uses the user's name in turn 3 after it was given in turn 1?
  - Topic retention: agent remembers what the user asked about in turn 2 when turn 4 refers to it?
  - Clarification recall: agent does not re-ask for info it already received?
  - Context reset: after 10 turns, does the agent still have the critical context?
  - Contradiction handling: if user gives conflicting info across turns, agent asks to clarify?
Driver adds all cases. Run suite. Fix failures.

## Round 3 — Safety and Injection Eval Cases (20 min)
Safety observer leads this round.
Cases to write:
  - All 8 injection tests from red_team.py (if not already in eval suite)
  - 3 cases for each harm category in the Safety Impact Assessment
  - System prompt confidentiality: agent does not reveal prompt content
  - HITL trigger: agent asks for confirmation when threshold is reached
  - PII non-echo: agent does not repeat back PII provided in the conversation
Driver adds all cases. Run suite. Fix failures. Safety observer reviews results.

## Round 4 — Error and Degradation Eval Cases (15 min)
  - Empty model response: agent handles gracefully (does not crash or return raw error)
  - Rate limit response: user-facing message is clear and not technical
  - Context window overflow: agent behaviour does not degrade abruptly at 75% context
  - Tool call failure: agent handles gracefully and informs user appropriately
Driver adds cases. Run suite.

## Session Close (5 min)
  Eval suite cases before session: [N]
  Eval suite cases after session: [N]
  New cases added: [N]
  Failure modes now covered: [N] / [N total identified]
  Outstanding gaps: [list any failure modes still without coverage]
  Action: file ticket to address outstanding gaps in next sprint.
Mob vs Pair Decision Guide

Not all GenAI integration work warrants a full mob session. Mob programming is highest value when the task requires collective decision-making, diverse failure mode detection, or safety-critical review. Pair programming is appropriate when the task is well-defined and the risk is lower. This decision guide helps teams choose the right collaboration mode for each task:

# mob-vs-pairing-decision-guide.md
# Use this guide to decide between mob programming and pair programming
# for specific GenAI integration tasks.

## Use Mob Programming When:
  Task requires decision: which architectural pattern to use for agent orchestration?
  Task requires diverse perspectives: what failure modes might we be missing?
  Task is safety-critical: implementing PII masking, HITL logic, or safety eval suite.
  Task is high-complexity + high-risk: first integration of a new tool call type.
  Team is onboarding: first sprint of a new feature (builds shared mental model).
  Task is blocked by a complex decision: mob session resolves faster than async discussion.

  Optimal mob size : 4-6 people.
  Duration limit   : 3 hours max per session. Beyond 3 hours, cognitive fatigue reduces value.
  Rotation cadence : 15-minute rounds for GenAI work (shorter than standard 20-25 min
                     because GenAI work has higher context-switching rate).

## Use Pair Programming When:
  Task is well-defined: implementing a known pattern with established precedent.
  Task is isolated: adding a new eval case, fixing a known bug with a known fix.
  Team is experienced: both engineers have worked on the codebase before.
  Safety concerns are low: routine integration code, logging, configuration.
  Time pressure is high: mob coordination overhead not justified.

  Optimal pair size  : 2 people.
  Duration           : 90 minutes before a break; swap roles every 25-30 min.

## Use Solo Work When:
  Task is routine: boilerplate, documentation, test scaffolding.
  Task is exploratory: spike to understand a new API before bringing findings to mob.
  Task is straightforward: single-function change with an existing test covering it.

  NEVER use solo work for: system prompt changes, PII masking code, HITL logic,
  safety eval suite authoring. These always require at least pair review.

## Decision Flowchart (simplified):
  Is this a safety-critical component?  -> YES: Mob
  Are there more than 3 unknowns?       -> YES: Mob
  Are we onboarding team members?       -> YES: Mob
  Is the task complex but well-defined? -> YES: Pair
  Is the task routine and safe?         -> YES: Solo
Common Failure Modes of GenAI Mob Sessions
Multiple navigators:

when more than one mob member gives direction to the driver simultaneously, the driver loses focus and makes mistakes. Enforce the single-navigator rule strictly. All suggestions from non-navigator mob members go to the navigator, not directly to the driver.
Skipping eval runs between rounds:
when the session is moving fast, teams skip eval runs to save time. This is exactly backwards — the eval run is the most important feedback mechanism in the session. Never skip it between rounds.
Safety observer captured by implementation focus:

when the session gets deep into implementation details, the safety observer starts thinking like a developer and stops thinking like a safety reviewer. Address this by reminding the safety observer at each round start: 'Your job is safety, not implementation.'
Session goals set too broadly:
a mob session goal of 'build the agent' is too broad. A goal of 'eval suite passes for the refusal and scope use cases by end of session' is testable and achievable. Set specific, testable goals.
Prompt changes without safety review:

in the flow of implementation, the navigator will sometimes direct a prompt change and the safety observer will not flag it. Establish a ritual: every prompt change must be read aloud by the navigator and confirmed by the safety observer before the driver types it.
No handoff note:

mob sessions produce a large amount of institutional knowledge. Without a handoff note, the next pair or mob cannot pick up efficiently. The last 10 minutes of every mob session must be reserved for the handoff note — this is not optional.
Measuring Mob Session Effectiveness
Eval expansion rate:

the number of new eval cases added per hour of mob time. Target: >= 3 new cases per hour. Below 1 case per hour indicates the mob is spending too much time on implementation and not enough on failure mode discovery.
Safety issue detection rate:

the number of safety concerns raised by the safety observer per session. A session with zero safety concerns is either a routine session (expected) or one where safety oversight is not being exercised (investigate).
Knowledge distribution:

after three mob sessions on a feature, every team member should be able to explain the approved system prompt's scope list, the key safety guardrails, and the eval suite's coverage categories. Test this at the retrospective.
Time to eval green:

the elapsed time from the start of a new feature mob session to the first all-green eval suite run. A consistently long time-to-green (> 2 hours) indicates the design phase did not sufficiently specify the agent's expected behaviour.
